title: OmniRobotHome: A Multi-Camera Platform for Real-Time Multiadic Human-Robot Interaction
abstract: 人机协作的研究主要集中在二元或顺序场景中。然而，真实家庭环境需要多主体协作——多个人类与机器人共享工作空间，并在时空紧密耦合的交错子任务中并行行动。这一领域尚未被充分探索，因为人类、机器人与物体之间的近距离交互会产生持续性遮挡和快速状态变化，使得可靠的实时三维追踪成为核心瓶颈。现有平台均无法提供实时、抗遮挡、房间级的三维感知能力来使该场景的实验变得可行。我们提出OmniRobotHome，这是首个将广域实时三维人体与物体的感知、与多机器人协同驱动统一在共享世界坐标系中的房间级住宅平台。该系统为自然家居环境部署了48台硬件同步的RGB相机，实现对多个人体与物体的无标记抗遮挡追踪，并与实时场景状态下运行的两台Franka机械臂实现时间对齐。在该一致坐标系中的连续捕获还能基于累积轨迹支持长时序人类行为建模。该平台使多主体协作场景的实验研究变得可行。我们聚焦两大核心问题：共享人机环境的安全性，以及具有人类预判能力的机器人辅助。研究表明，实时感知与累积行为记忆各自都能在这两个问题上带来可量化的性能提升。

OmniRobotHome
: A Multi-Camera Platform for Real-Time Multiadic Human-Robot Interaction
Junyoung Lee
1∗
Sookwan Han
1∗

Abstract
Human-robot collaboration has been studied primarily in dyadic or sequential settings. However, real homes require multiadic collaboration, where multiple humans and robots share a workspace, acting concurrently on interleaved subtasks with tight spatial and temporal coupling. This regime remains underexplored because close-proximity interaction between humans, robots, and objects creates persistent occlusion and rapid state changes, making reliable real-time 3D tracking the central bottleneck. No existing platform provides the real-time, occlusion-robust, room-scale perception needed to make this regime experimentally tractable.
We present
OmniRobotHome
, the first room-scale residential platform that unifies wide-area real-time 3D human and object perception with coordinated multi-robot actuation in a shared world frame. The system instruments a natural home environment with 48 hardware-synchronized RGB cameras for markerless, occlusion-robust tracking of multiple humans and objects, temporally aligned with two Franka arms that act on live scene state. Continuous capture within this consistent frame further supports long-horizon human behavior modeling from accumulated trajectories.
The platform makes the multiadic collaboration regime experimentally tractable. We focus on two central problems: safety in shared human-robot environments and human-anticipatory robotic assistance, and show that real-time perception and accumulated behavior memory each yield measurable gains in both.
1
Introduction
Enabling robots to assist with or replace human labor in everyday living environments remains a fundamental objective in robotics. Unlike structured industrial workspaces or constrained tabletop scenarios, household environments are inherently unstructured and dynamic. Humans move freely, interact with diverse objects, and continuously reconfigure their surroundings. A system that observes, understands, and anticipates such behavior can enable far more intelligent robotic assistance. Yet practical home scenarios go beyond a single robot assisting a single person: many tasks require multiple robots and humans to share the same workspace, acting concurrently on interleaved subtasks with tight spatial and temporal coupling—a regime we refer to as multiadic collaboration. Studying this regime requires distributed real-time perception that spans the full living space, tightly coupled with robotic hardware so that assistance is conditioned on live human and object state. The central bottleneck is reliable multi-agent 3D tracking: close-proximity interaction between humans, robots, and objects creates persistent occlusion and rapid state changes, making real-time, occlusion-robust, room-scale perception the critical missing capability.
Most prior robotics research assumes constrained environments with localized sensing
[
brohan2023RT1
,
kalashnikov2018QTOpt
]
, particularly tabletop settings where perception is simplified and surrounding human motion is not explicitly modeled
[
shridhar2022CLIPort
,
zeng2021Transporter
]
. More recent work explores mobile robots in larger spaces
[
wu2023TidyBot
,
liu2024OKRobot
]
, but sensing remains designed for navigation, not continuous human monitoring. On the other hand, computer vision research has advanced large-scale human sensing through multi-camera 3D motion capture
[
joo2016Panoptic
,
kim2025ParaHomea
]
, but most such systems target offline processing or dataset construction and rarely support real-time operation
[
mahmood2019AMASS
,
grauman2024EgoExo4D
]
. Platforms that provide the real-time, occlusion-robust perception needed to study multiadic collaboration under close-proximity interaction remain largely unexplored. Without such a platform, the core research questions of this regime—how to maintain safety under concurrent operation, how to infer human intent for timely handovers, and how to coordinate simultaneous actions with shared dependencies—have remained beyond experimental reach.
We present
OmniRobotHome
, a room-scale physical AI testbed that removes this bottleneck and enables systematic study of multiadic human-robot collaboration in a natural home environment. The system continuously perceives human activity and object state across the full living space, and provides coordinated multi-robot assistance conditioned on the shared, real-time 3D scene state, through four key capabilities.
(1) Large sensing coverage in a unified world frame.
The system tracks human location and full-body pose across a wide living space as the person moves naturally, supporting analysis of trajectories, behavioral patterns, and activity intent. A single consistent world frame shared by all cameras and robot arms provides the spatial foundation for multi-agent coordination and safety policies.
(2) Real-time markerless multi-human and multi-object 3D perception.
Unlike conventional multi-camera systems designed for offline processing, our system performs real-time markerless multi-human tracking with 6D object pose estimation. This allows robot actions to be conditioned on live human and object state rather than replayed data, enabling reactive safety and timing-sensitive interaction.
(3) Multi-robot actuation.
Two robot arms are deployed within the environment, temporally aligned with the shared perception stream. This enables multiadic interaction scenarios where multiple robots act concurrently with task-coupled dependencies on the human and on each other, and broadens the available workspace.
(4) Human behavior memory.
Continuous markerless capture within a consistent world frame enables long-term accumulation of behavioral data from residents, supporting modeling of habitual routines. Accumulated trajectories become priors that collaboration policies consume directly, enabling anticipatory assistance that improves with observation time.
Together, these four capabilities make
OmniRobotHome
the first platform that simultaneously provides room-scale real-time human and object sensing in 3D with coordinated multi-arm robotic assistance in a unified residential environment. Building such a system introduces substantial engineering challenges in calibration, synchronization, networking, and distributed processing; this paper presents practical solutions to each. More importantly, the platform makes multiadic collaboration experimentally tractable, enabling controlled studies that isolate each capability’s contribution. We focus on two central problems: (1)
safety in shared human-robot environments
: we demonstrate that real-time sensing and predictive modeling of human motion significantly improve safety when humans and robots coexist; and (2)
human-anticipatory robotic assistance
: we evaluate whether behavioral observation enables intent inference for anticipatory assistance, from placement prediction to pose-conditioned handovers.
Our contributions are: (1) a room-scale residential platform with 48 hardware-synchronized cameras and two robot arms; (2) an end-to-end real-time sensing pipeline for perception, data acquisition, and human motion prediction; and (3) systematic human-robot interaction studies in multiadic residential environments demonstrating that wide-area sensing, real-time perception, and long-term behavioral modeling each contribute measurable gains to safety and assistive performance. All code and data will be publicly released.
2
Related Work
2.1
Room-Scale Human and Object Sensing
Dense multi-view coverage is required to recover 3D human and object state under occlusion. Panoptic Studio
[
joo2016Panoptic
]
scaled markerless capture to social interaction, and subsequent systems extended coverage to hand-object grasping
[
taheri2020GRAB
]
, whole-body interaction in furnished rooms
[
kim2025ParaHomea
]
, and joint human-object trajectories
[
hassan2019Resolving
,
lu2025HUMOTO
]
. All operate offline and none produces real-time 3D scene state for closed-loop robotic control.
2.2
Robotic Manipulation Platforms
Platforms for robot learning have scaled in task diversity and data volume. ALOHA
[
zhao2023Learning
,
fu2024Mobile
]
demonstrates bimanual skills via teleoperation, SayCan
[
ahn2022Can
]
grounds language in affordances, and large datasets
[
khazatsky2025DROID
,
collaboration2025Open
,
fang2023RH20T
]
aggregate trajectories across embodiments. Multi-robot coordination has progressed from industrial assembly
[
yamada1995Development
]
to collision-free multi-arm planning
[
ka2024Systematic
,
lai2025RoboBallet
]
and multi-user evaluation
[
ye2025M4Bench
]
. These platforms treat human state as external to the control loop; none integrates room-scale real-time human and object sensing with coordinated multi-robot actuation.
2.3
Human-Robot Interaction and Safety
Collaboration in shared workspaces requires intention understanding, real-time adaptation, and proximity-aware safety. Handover methods condition on predicted contact
[
wang2024ContactHandover
]
, receiver motion
[
kim2025Learningbased
]
, and gaze
[
moon2014Meeta
]
. Cooperative planners model human motion
[
choi2017Nonparametric
]
, learn interaction primitives
[
ewerton2015Learning
]
, predict intention from skeleton cues
[
solak2025Contextaware
]
, and learn strategies via imitation
[
wang2023CoGAIL
]
. For safety, reactive
[
ratliff2018Riemannian
,
bhardwaj2021STORM
]
and learning-based
[
fishman2025Avoid
,
yang2025Deep
]
controllers handle collision avoidance in dynamic settings. Existing work overwhelmingly studies dyadic interaction with onboard or single-viewpoint sensing; multiadic collaboration with task-coupled dependencies remains largely unexplored.
2.4
Human Motion Prediction and Behavior Memory
Proactive assistance requires anticipating human actions. Short-horizon prediction forecasts body trajectory seconds ahead, enabling preemptive collision avoidance and timely handovers. Trajectory forecasting
[
salzmann2020Trajectron
,
mao2020History
]
has advanced substantially, with InteRACT
[
kedia2024InteRACT
]
modeling how predicted intent shifts in response to robot actions. Long-horizon modeling captures routines from accumulated data: personalized systems
[
jenamani2025FEAST
,
wang2025MOSAIC
]
adapt to preferences over sessions, with surveys identifying prediction as central to proactive collaboration
[
li2023Proactive
,
xia2025Human
]
. Both horizons have been developed in constrained dyadic settings with localized sensors; no prior work combines continuous room-scale capture with accumulated behavioral data for anticipatory multiadic assistance.

:
System overview of
OmniRobotHome
.
48 hardware-synchronized cameras across 12 edge nodes provide real-time markerless 3D perception of humans, objects, and robots in a unified world frame. Details in

3
System Design
3.1
Overview
Exploring multiadic human-robot collaboration requires real-time, occlusion-robust, room-scale 3D perception tightly coupled with robotic actuation.
OmniRobotHome
provides this through a synchronized sensing and control architecture (
Fig.
˜
1
).
We instrument a 23.1 m
2
residential space with 48 hardware-synchronized RGB cameras operating at 30 Hz. The cameras are distributed across 12 edge nodes that run real-time person detection and 2D pose estimation in parallel. A central server triangulates 3D whole-body keypoints and estimates object 6D poses, producing a unified scene state in a shared world frame. This scene state is temporally aligned with two Franka Research 3 arms, enabling multi-robot actuation conditioned on live human and object state. Continuous capture within this consistent frame further accumulates behavioral data over time, providing trajectory priors that improve both safety responses and anticipatory assistance.
3.2
Hardware Configuration
Capture Space Setup.
Our capture space spans
23.1
​
m
2
23.1\,\mathrm{m}^{2}
and is instrumented with 48 RGB cameras to provide dense visual coverage of the entire space and reduce occlusions. Among those, we set 2 cameras placed as a stereo pair facing toward the tabletop for 6D object pose tracking.
Robot Setup.
Our system deploys two Franka Research 3 arms, each equipped with a parallel-jaw gripper at the end-effector.
Hardware Synchronization.
All RGB cameras are synchronized via an external hardware trigger. We extract timestamps from a reference camera to trigger the robot’s data reception, ensuring that all detection and reconstruction outputs are temporally aligned.
Figure 2
:
Real-Time Perception.
(a) Distributed human pose estimation: each of 12 edge nodes runs YOLO-based detection and RTMPose for 2D whole-body keypoints; a central server triangulates 3D joints via RANSAC multi-view reconstruction.
(b) Object 6D pose estimation from a calibrated stereo pair. Meshes are precomputed via MV-SAM3D
[
sam3dteam2025sam3d3dfyimages
]
(top); at runtime, FoundationStereo provides metric depth and FoundationPose performs marker-free 6D tracking at 6 Hz (bottom).
(c) Live system operation with concurrent human activity and robotic actuation.
(d) Human pose throughput and per-module latency vs. number of tracked humans (40 cameras).
(e) Object 6D tracking throughput and per-module latency vs. number of tracked objects (1 stereo pair).
3.3
System Calibration
Placing 48 cameras and two robot arms in a shared world frame requires three calibration stages. First, each camera is intrinsically calibrated with ChArUco boards
[
garrido-jurado2014Automatic
]
using Zhang’s method
[
zhang2000Flexible
]
. Second, we recover extrinsic poses by solving PnP
[
Grunert1841P3P
,
QuanLan1999LinearNP
]
across 16 distributed ChArUco boards, unifying all cameras into a single world frame via a bipartite pose graph, and refining jointly with bundle adjustment over board poses, camera poses, and optionally intrinsics. This custom pipeline outperforms COLMAP
[
schonberger2016StructurefromMotion
]
in our setting because it avoids accuracy degradation in regions without calibration patterns. Third, each robot base is registered into the camera world frame via hand-eye calibration
[
tsai1989New
]
, placing the full kinematic chain in the unified coordinate frame. Detailed formulations are provided in the supplementary material.
3.4
Real-Time Perception
Fig.
˜
2
summarizes the two perception pipelines and their throughput as a function of scene complexity.
Multi-View Human Pose Estimation.
The 48 cameras generate approximately 13.5 GB/s of raw data. We handle this with a distributed architecture: each of 12 edge nodes runs TensorRT-optimized YOLO26
[
yolo26_ultralytics
]
(INT8) for detection and RTMPose
[
jiang2023RTMPose
]
(FP16) for 2D whole-body keypoint estimation following COCO WholeBody
[
jin2020WholeBody
]
, streaming only keypoints and confidence scores to a central server. The server triangulates 3D joints via RANSAC-based multi-view reconstruction in a parallel process and applies a One Euro Filter
[
casiez20121
]
for temporal smoothing, delivering stable whole-body pose estimates to the robot controller.
Object 6D Pose Estimation.
Our system uses only RGB cameras, as dedicated depth sensors lack the hardware synchronization support needed across 48 streams. We adopt a fully learned pipeline on a calibrated stereo pair: FoundationStereo
[
wen2025FoundationStereo
]
(TensorRT-optimized) provides dense metric depth, YOLO-E
[
yolo26_ultralytics
]
generates segmentation masks, and FoundationPose
[
wen2024FoundationPose
]
performs marker-free 6D tracking from a template mesh alone. For objects without CAD models, we reconstruct meshes offline using MV-SAM3D
[
sam3dteam2025sam3d3dfyimages
]
from our dense camera array and align them to the world frame via multi-view silhouette reprojection. The pipeline runs at approximately 6 Hz, sufficient for the typical velocities of manipulated objects.
4
Experiments
The capabilities of
OmniRobotHome
(large sensing coverage, real-time markerless tracking, multi-robot actuation, and human behavior memory) open research questions in multiadic collaboration that were previously beyond experimental reach. We focus on two central practical problems:
safety in shared human-robot environments
(
Tab.
˜
1
) and
human-anticipatory robotic assistance
(
Tab.
˜
2
), with ablations on the impact of human-behavior memory (
Fig.
˜
4
). Unified real-time perception and multi-robot actuation also support tighter coupling, which we qualitatively demonstrate via
intention-aware transfer
(
Fig.
˜
5
). We further validate key system components, including calibration accuracy and camera count (
Fig.
˜
6
).
4.1
Research Questions
We ground both problems in a shared kitchen organization scenario. Food items are distributed across a table and kitchen surfaces, and two robot arms must concurrently sort each item to its correct destination; fresh produce to the sink, packaged goods to shelves; while a human moves freely through the workspace. The two arms divide the workload across spatially separated regions, perceive scene state through the shared real-time 3D stream, and must cooperate with each other (e.g., handing off items between table and kitchen) and with the human throughout. This setup stresses all four system capabilities: room-scale coverage localizes the human and objects across the full space, real-time tracking feeds live 3D state to both arms, multi-robot actuation broadens the workspace to kitchen and table via cooperative handovers, and behavior memory provides priors on human routines.
Safety in shared workspaces.
The core question is whether real-time 3D human-state conditioning enables policies that are simultaneously safe and productive, and whether accumulated behavioral history can further improve safety by anticipating human motion before it occurs. Room-scale coverage ensures the human is always observed; real-time perception lets each arm react instantly; behavior memory enables preemptive avoidance. We replay recorded human trajectories in our custom simulation environment, comparing four safety policies of increasing sophistication and one augmented with behavior learning. We measure collision count and cycle time (
Tab.
˜
1
), analyze behavior memory’s effect on anticipatory safety (
Fig.
˜
4
), and demonstrate the performance in real (
Fig.
˜
3
(a)).
Figure 3
:
Task Overview.
(a) Safety-aware coexistence: two arms sort food items in a shared kitchen while a human moves freely; each arm yields, reroutes, or pauses based on real-time 3D human state.
(b) Human-anticipatory assistance: given partial demonstrations of a human sorting objects, the robot must infer the placement rule and predict the correct destination for each remaining item.
Human-anticipatory assistance.
The question is whether the robot can infer a human’s sorting intent from partial observation from our system and generalize to unseen categories. We frame this as a placement prediction task: given that the human has placed
k
k
of 12 objects (6 fruits to the sink, 6 packaged goods to the shelf), the robot must predict the correct destination for each remaining item. We compare a lookup baseline, which copies exact observed placements, against two LLM-based methods, a direct model (LLM) and one augmented with chain-of-thought reasoning (+ Reasoning), both receiving demonstrations as text via domain-neutral prompts without category hints. We measure correct placement ratio as a function of observed demonstration count (
Tab.

Fig.

We present results for both central problems. All quantitative experiments use the kitchen organization scenario described above.

The non-aware baseline confirms that speed without human-state conditioning is unsafe, incurring over five times more collisions than even the least restrictive conditioned policy. Static-radius policies expose a fundamental safety-throughput tradeoff: tighter radii preserve throughput but leave many collisions, while conservative radii nearly eliminate collisions at the cost of severe slowdowns. The dynamic policy breaks this tradeoff by conditioning on real-time 3D human state, reducing collisions to 38% of the tight static radius while improving cycle time. Adding behavior learning further reduces collisions with no cycle-time penalty, showing that accumulated trajectory history enables the policy to anticipate human motion before it enters the operating region.

We compare two LLM-based methods against a lookup baseline. Lookup retrieves the most recent recorded placement for each object from the behavioral database, without learning or generalization. At zero demonstrations, both LLM variants slightly outperform Lookup, reflecting residual category knowledge despite the no-prior-knowledge instruction. All LLM methods reach 100% accuracy at half the demonstration sequence, showing that partial behavioral history suffices to disambiguate the placement rule. Lookup climbs linearly and only reaches full accuracy at the complete sequence, since it can only reproduce placements it has explicitly observed.

Accumulated behavior memory improves both safety and intent-aware assistance, validating that continuous markerless capture in a consistent world frame produces actionable priors.
Safety (

(a)) improves sharply with even a small amount of recorded data: the first episodes capture the dominant human approach patterns and already halve the collision count. Collision counts briefly rise between roughly 15% and 35% of the data before resuming their decline. We attribute this to a small number of atypical trajectories in the training window; additional data dilutes these outliers and the downward trend resumes. Cycle time varies by under 1% across this range, indicating that the safety gain carries no measurable throughput cost.

Safety and intent prediction draw on the behavior memory differently. Safety stabilizes once the dominant approach patterns are captured, while intent prediction only converges once demonstrations span both object categories.

These individualized profiles enable the system to deliver personalized safety responses and anticipatory assistance, rather than relying on a single population-level model.

4.3
Qualitative Demonstrations
Beyond the kitchen scenario, we demonstrate intention-aware transfer with increasing coupling between agents (
Fig.
˜
5
). The system relies on real-time 3D human pose and object state from the multi-view system and uses a VLM to infer human intent from scene context.
Intention-aware transfer.
The robot must infer which object a human needs and deliver it with timing conditioned on real-time pose. The pipeline proceeds in three stages: (1) the system detects the human in the workspace via multi-view 3D tracking; (2) a VLM infers the target object from the human’s 3D pose and surrounding scene context (objects and spatial configuration around the human); and (3) the robot executes a pick-handover-place sequence from the shelf to the human’s hand, with the handover target continuously updated from the human’s real-time 3D hand keypoints rather than committed to a fixed pose. The transfer initiates when short-horizon cues indicate readiness and delays when the human disengages. We demonstrate three scenarios (
Fig.
˜
5
): (a) a human notices a dry plant and the robot delivers a watering kettle, (b) a human prepares to eat a hot dog and the robot hands over a mustard bottle, and (c) a human eating chips gestures for a drink and the robot delivers a soft drink. In all cases, the system successfully identifies the needed object from pose and scene cues and adapts the handover trajectory online as the human’s reach evolves.

System Ablations
We validate core system components quantitatively: calibration accuracy against a standard baseline, and the effect of camera count on pose coverage and reconstruction quality.
Calibration accuracy (
Tab.
˜
3
).
We compare our custom ChArUco-based calibration pipeline against COLMAP, both evaluated with markers captured at multiple random positions in the capture space. Our pipeline reduces the mean reprojection error from 1.42 px to 1.21 px and the standard deviation from 1.09 px to 0.80 px, indicating both lower bias and tighter consistency across camera views. When we reproject every triangulated 3D corner back into its observing camera views, 48.9% of reprojections land within 1 px of their detected location (vs. 43.9% for COLMAP), and 86.8% within 2 px (vs. 77.3%).

5
Conclusion
We presented
OmniRobotHome
, a room-scale residential platform that makes multiadic human-robot collaboration experimentally tractable. The system instruments a 23.1 m
2
home environment with 48 hardware-synchronized RGB cameras for markerless, occlusion-robust 3D tracking of multiple humans and objects in a unified world frame, temporally aligned with two robot arms for multi-robot actuation conditioned on live scene state. Continuous capture within this consistent frame further accumulates behavioral trajectories that the system converts into priors for safer and more anticipatory assistance.
Controlled studies on two central problems isolate the contribution of each capability. For safety in shared workspaces, conditioning on real-time 3D human state breaks the safety-throughput tradeoff that static-radius policies cannot resolve, reducing collisions by 2.6 times at improved cycle time, and accumulated behavior memory further reduces collisions with no throughput penalty by anticipating human motion before it occurs. For human-anticipatory assistance, partial observation of a resident’s behavioral history already disambiguates intent: the system reaches full placement accuracy from half the demonstration sequence, while a copy baseline requires the complete sequence. Behavior memory reveals complementary dynamics across the two problems: safety benefits from a compact early observation window that captures dominant movement patterns, while intent prediction requires demonstrations spanning both object categories before converging. Qualitative demonstrations of intention-aware transfer extend the coupling further: the system infers the target object from real-time 3D human pose and scene context and adapts the handover trajectory online as the human’s reach evolves. System ablations confirm that the infrastructure supports these results, with calibration achieving 1.21 px mean reprojection error versus 1.42 px for COLMAP and 48 cameras providing over 80% volumetric coverage across the full room. Across both problems, the common enabling factor is continuous room-scale perception in a unified world frame: real-time 3D state drives reactive safety and timing-sensitive interaction, while accumulated behavioral priors improve anticipatory performance, together making the multiadic regime observable and actionable for the first time.
Limitations and future work.
Our evaluation uses a single instrumented room with two fixed-base arms and task-specific controllers; generalization across layouts, embodiments, and control interfaces remains open. Future work includes integrating mobile robots, expanding to diverse room configurations, and releasing synchronized multiadic sequences with calibrated 3D trajectories, object poses, robot states, and task annotations. These data can potentially support further studies in human-robot interaction, such as training human-aware vision-language-action
[
kim2024openvla
]
models.

Hardware Details
Camera configuration.
The perception system comprises a synchronized network of 48 industrial RGB cameras (FLIR Blackfly S BFLY-PGE-31S4C-C) capturing
2048

1536

Synchronization and data acquisition.
Frame-level synchronization is enforced via a hardware trigger (10 Vpp square wave, 50% duty cycle) generated by a UNI-T UTG962 unit and distributed through a hierarchical chain of 8-port GPIO signal distributors.
Data acquisition is distributed across 12 capture nodes connected via GigE Vision interfaces, each managing four cameras. Ten nodes are dedicated to pose estimation (40 cameras), while two nodes each host two calibrated stereo pairs for 6D object pose estimation (8 cameras). A dedicated server aggregates all data streams in real-time. Each capture node is equipped with dual local SSDs (500 GB + 1 TB) for high-bandwidth buffering, and 15 LED panels (4500 lm each) provide uniform illumination throughout the workspace.

Our human detection pipeline operates on a distributed client-server architecture, where each capture node independently performs 2D pose estimation and transmits results to a central server for multi-view 3D triangulation.

Each capture node processes frames from its designated cameras through a two-stage detection pipeline. First, a TensorRT-optimized YOLO26
[

]

384
×
288

[
jiang2023RTMPose
]

[
li2022simcc

Data Aggregation.
Clients transmit keypoint data to the server via ZMQ PUSH/PULL sockets using msgpack serialization. The server tracks the liveness of each camera and promotes a frame for triangulation only when at least 60% of the active pose cameras (minimum 4) report data within a 60-frame window, ensuring sufficient multi-view coverage.

0.B.2.2
Execution architecture
Dynamic policy generation.
We generate runtime policies for each robot arm through template substitution.
Each template contains placeholder waypoints for the handover and prehandover phases.
At execution time, these are replaced with category-specific poses precomputed offline (
Sec.
˜
0.B.2.1
), producing policies specialized to the detected object class.
This bridges offline optimization with real-time execution without performing workspace search or bimanual IK online.
Persistent daemon execution.
Each robot is controlled by a persistent C++ daemon that maintains robot and gripper connections across cycles.
A daemon supports policy reload without reconnecting, stepwise execution that advances one policy step on command, and synchronization signaling through interprocess markers.
This reduces cycle overhead and enables rapid switching across object categories.
Dual-arm synchronization.
PHP enforces two sync points:
Sync A
at prehandover, ensuring aligned approach timing, and
Sync B
at handover, initiating the gripper exchange.
At
Sync B
, the receiver closes first; after a fixed delay the giver opens, ensuring the object is secured before release.
Asynchronous detection scheduling.
We overlap perception and manipulation by triggering the next object detection at
Sync B
, while both robots are stationary and the grasp exchange is ongoing.
This timing reduces occlusion in camera views and hides perception latency behind manipulation time.
Detection runs asynchronously in the background, and the most recent 6D pose is consumed for the next pick.

Rate limiting.
All commanded Cartesian and joint velocities are rate-limited per axis at the servo loop rate, producing smooth acceleration profiles and reducing reflex triggers from sudden command jumps.
Defensive clearing and reflex handling.
Before each motion segment, the executor stops residual motion and clears pending error states.
Reflex errors are caught, reported to the orchestrator, and recovery proceeds only under bounded retries.
Fallback motion.
Human-avoid requests interrupt motion smoothly and move the robot to a predefined fallback configuration via lock-free atomic flags, allowing the servo loop to react immediately.
Grasp-exchange exclusion.
Human-avoid interruptions are blocked for the full duration of the grasp exchange using explicit state flags, ensuring the object is never released without a secure grip.

0.B.6
Intention-Aware Transfer Pipeline
The preceding sections address robot-to-robot object transfer; this section
tackles the complementary problem of delivering an object to a human based on
inferred intention.
Because the multi-camera infrastructure provides both real-time 3D human body
tracking and 6D object pose estimation concurrently, the robot can reason
jointly about
what
the person needs and
where
to deliver it.
We describe a fully automated pipeline: once the user issues a trigger,
multi-view perception, intention inference, object selection, and physical
delivery proceed without further human intervention.
Upon receiving the trigger signal, the system executes the following sequence:
(1) multi-view image capture,
(2) 3D human body pose extraction via triangulation,
(3) vision-language model (VLM) based intention inference,
(4) object identification, and
(5) Pick-Handover-Place (PHP) execution with the handover target set to the
human’s hand.
0.B.6.1
Multi-view scene representation
Inferring human intention from a single viewpoint is inherently ambiguous:
occlusion, oblique perspectives, and limited spatial context can all lead to
incorrect readings of the person’s gaze, gesture, or surrounding objects.
To mitigate these issues, we select six camera views from the 48-camera array
and composite them into a single
2
×
3
2\!\times\!3
grid image.
The resulting mosaic provides comprehensive spatial coverage of the person’s
posture, gaze direction, and environmental context from multiple complementary
angles.
Among the six views, a single view specifically captures the tabletop
region where graspable candidate objects are arranged, allowing the VLM to
observe both the person’s ongoing activity and the available objects within a
single input frame.
0.B.6.2
3D human body keypoints
In addition to the visual grid, we supply the VLM with quantitative geometric
cues derived from 3D human body tracking.
Our multi-view system reconstructs 16 body keypoints—from pelvis to
wrists—in world coordinates via multi-view triangulation.
These keypoints compactly encode body pose, facing direction, hand extension,
and spatial location, complementing the qualitative visual information with
structured numerical input.
0.B.6.3
VLM-based chain-of-thought inference
We employ GPT-5.2 with high-effort reasoning to perform structured intention
inference.
The model receives three inputs: the six-view grid image, the 3D body
keypoints, and a predefined candidate list of 23 everyday items spanning food,
kitchenware, household tools, and personal belongings.
Each candidate has a corresponding 3D mesh generated through an automated
reconstruction pipeline using MV-SAM3D
[
sam3dteam2025sam3d3dfyimages
]
.
Rather than directly predicting the target object, we instruct the model to
reason in five explicit steps:
(i)
identify which candidates are physically present on the tabletop
and thus reachable by the robot;
(ii)
describe the person’s current activity by examining all six
viewpoints holistically;
(iii)
infer the person’s underlying intention from gaze, gesture, body
pose, and situational context;
(iv)
rank the top five most plausible objects from the reachable subset;
and
(v)
select the single best object with contrastive reasoning explaining
why each runner-up is less suitable.
This chain-of-thought formulation grounds the selection in observable evidence
rather than generic priors, and the intermediate outputs—reachable list,
activity description, and intention analysis—provide interpretable
justification for the final decision.
0.B.6.4
Handover execution
Once the target object is identified, the PHP pipeline grasps it from the
tabletop using 6D pose estimation and delivers it to the human.
The handover target pose is continuously updated using the person’s real-time
3D hand keypoints from the multi-view tracking system.
Because full-body tracking runs concurrently, the delivery destination
naturally adapts as the person moves, enabling fluid handover without
predefined waypoints or static target positions.

preview_text:
Human-robot collaboration has been studied primarily in dyadic or sequential settings. However, real homes require multiadic collaboration, where multiple humans and robots share a workspace, acting concurrently on interleaved subtasks with tight spatial and temporal coupling. This regime remains underexplored because close-proximity interaction between humans, robots, and objects creates persistent occlusion and rapid state changes, making reliable real-time 3D tracking the central bottleneck. No existing platform provides the real-time, occlusion-robust, room-scale perception needed to make this regime experimentally tractable. We present OmniRobotHome, the first room-scale residential platform that unifies wide-area real-time 3D human and object perception with coordinated multi-robot actuation in a shared world frame. The system instruments a natural home environment with 48 hardware-synchronized RGB cameras for markerless, occlusion-robust tracking of multiple humans and objects, temporally aligned with two Franka arms that act on live scene state. Continuous capture within this consistent frame further supports long-horizon human behavior modeling from accumulated trajectories. The platform makes the multiadic collaboration regime experimentally tractable. We focus on two central problems: safety in shared human-robot environments and human-anticipatory robotic assistance, and show that real-time perception and accumulated behavior memory each yield measurable gains in both.
